sentences.base_language: I just got some great news!
sentences.target_language: たった今、素晴らしい知らせが届きました！
sentences.romanized: Tatta ima, subarashii shirase ga todokimashita!
sentences.grammarNote: たった今 (tatta ima) = 'just now / this very moment', emphasizing extreme recency. 届く (todoku) = 'to arrive / reach (a message or item)'. The agent is omitted — the news 'arrived' without specifying who sent it, a natural Japanese construction.

〜される (sareru) is the passive form: 採用する (to hire) → 採用される (to be hired). Japanese often uses the passive to describe receiving an action. The sentence-final ました reports the event as completed — similar to the Chinese 'new situation' 了.

sentences.base_language: I'm so happy I could cry.
sentences.target_language: 嬉しくて泣きそうです。
sentences.grammarNote: 〜くて (kute) for い-adjectives creates a causal link: 嬉しくて (because I'm happy / so happy that…). 泣きそう = 'on the verge of crying / looks like I might cry'. The 〜そう suffix on a verb stem expresses something is about to happen.

sentences.base_language: My whole family is very proud of me.
sentences.target_language: 家族全員が私のことをとても誇りに思っています。
sentences.romanized: Kazoku zen'in ga watashi no koto wo totemo hokori ni omotte imasu.
sentences.literal: Family all me about very proud think.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜のことを (no koto wo) = 'about / regarding [a person]'. Adding のこと after a person's name/pronoun makes the reference more complete: 私のことを誇りに思う (be proud of me). It adds emotional depth — not just the facts but the whole matter of 'me'.

sentences.target_language: お祝いしなければなりません！
sentences.grammarNote: 〜なければなりません (nakerebanarimasen) = 'must / have to'. お祝い (oiwai) = 'celebration / congratulations'. The お prefix adds politeness. Casual alternative: お祝いしないと！ (we have to celebrate!) or お祝いしよう！ (let's celebrate!).

sentences.base_language: Let's go to dinner to celebrate.
sentences.target_language: お祝いに食事に行きましょう。
sentences.grammarNote: 〜に行く (ni iku) = 'go for the purpose of'. Here there are two に: お祝いに (for the purpose of celebrating) and 食事に行く (go to eat). The first に marks purpose/reason, the second marks the purpose of movement.

sentences.base_language: This is the best day of my life.
sentences.target_language: 人生で一番幸せな日です。
sentences.romanized: Jinsei de ichiban shiawase na hi desu.
sentences.literal: Life in number-one happy day is.

sentences.base_language: I worked really hard for this.
sentences.target_language: このために一生懸命頑張りました。
sentences.grammarNote: 一生懸命 (isshōkenmei) = 'with all one's might / with great effort / wholeheartedly'. 頑張る (ganbaru) = 'to do one's best / persevere'. These two together are among the most culturally important words in Japanese — expressing the value placed on effort and dedication.
